topic: Area of Study 3: Music for Media
subject: music

items.q: What makes a piece of music in a film 'diegetic'?
items.correct: It exists inside the story world, so the characters can hear it - for example a song playing on a car radio.
items.misconception: Diegetic means any music that was specially composed for the film.
items.random_wrong: Diegetic means music performed only on acoustic instruments.
items.why: The diegetic/non-diegetic distinction is about whether the sound source belongs to the story world, not about who composed the music or how it was made.

items.q: Does a leitmotif have to return in exactly the same form each time?
items.correct: No - composers vary its orchestration, harmony, tempo and mood so the theme reflects the changing drama.
items.misconception: A leitmotif must be repeated identically or the audience will not recognise it.
items.random_wrong: A leitmotif may only ever be played by the brass section.
items.why: The power of the leitmotif technique lies in transformation: audiences recognise the melodic shape while the changed setting tells them how the character or situation has developed.

items.q: How prominent should underscore be during dialogue?
items.correct: It usually sits low in the mix, supporting the mood without competing with the spoken words.
items.misconception: Underscore should be the loudest element so the audience notices the composer's work.
items.random_wrong: Underscore is always completely silent during dialogue.
items.why: Underscore is functional music: its job is to support the storytelling. Mixing it too high masks dialogue, which is why composers and mixers deliberately keep it understated.

items.q: Music that the on-screen characters can themselves hear is described as what?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Diegetic. “Non-diegetic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A recurring theme associated with a particular character in a film score is called a what?
items.why: It's Leitmotif. “Cadenza” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Music that precisely mirrors on-screen physical action is known as what?
items.why: Correct answer: Mickey-mousing. “Spotting” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What makes video-game music different from most film music?
items.correct: It must adapt in real time to the player's actions
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It must adapt in real time to the player's actions. “It never uses an orchestra” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A film cue at 120 bpm in 4/4 must last exactly 30 seconds. How many bars is that?
items.why: Exact answer: 15 bars. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
